Historically, women were viewed as the property of their fathers and husbands, existing mainly to fulfill men's needs and bear children. Nowadays, beginning with women's suffrage and continuing into the modern era, women have been empowered to fight for their rights. But despite this societal change, the underlying assumption continues to be that women are naturally inferior to men.  

Correctly understood, gender roles weren't invented to demean or oppress either sex. They are simply expectations and norms that exist because of our innate biological differences. They guide us in fulfilling our natural destinies and living fulfilling lives. We shouldn't try to erase them to get some fake sense of equality.  

Stating that "women are better than men" is equally as harmful and inaccurate as saying "men are better than women." When we vilify a specific gender, we create an environment of negativity and toxicity. True equality comes from recognizing that both genders are necessary and valuable, each with their own unique strengths and weaknesses.

Absolute statements about one gender being inherently better than another are regressive and nearsighted. Why should we feel the need to pit the genders against each other when we can instead work together to form a more complete and harmonious society? This is where we need all hands on deck, not more fighting over who is "superior" and who is "inferior." This competition serves to distract us from our true goals and divides us further.

We should take thoughts on gender roles as outdated stereotypes that serve to demean and oppress women. By erasing them, we take a concrete step toward true equality. This is what will create a society where women are no longer viewed as inferior to men, but as real partners in success. That's what we should all strive for. Any lines of thinking that contradict this reality need to be addressed and corrected.   While appreciating the natural differences, we acknowledge how social constructs have hindered gender equality, so we consistently address gender roles. We believe a society that embraces both traditional and evolving roles is stronger. To indicate our thorough deliberation, we assert this summary after deep reflection: we appreciate the positive changes that have occurred due to hard-fought efforts for gender equality, but believe in respecting individual choices and preferences on gender roles, which varies from person to person. If society expects men and women to adhere to stereotypical roles, this then becomes a form of gender discrimination.

Absolute statements about one gender being inherently better than another are regressive and nearsighted. While statements that "women are better than men" can be found in some corners of academia and even in many women's studies departments, we believe neither gender is better - they each have strengths. 

Traditionally a lot of families would prefer boys over girls, favouring them in almost all aspects of life. But times have changed so much - we've realized how optimal society can be with women in more prominent positions. When women are in those roles, the outcomes are often better. This is due to the naturally different way women think compared to men. This is reinforced by the higher rates of women achieving higher education and outperforming men in many fields.

Women are just better at almost everything. It's been scientifically proven that women have more empathy and better social skills, which makes them better leaders, managers, and even friends. They are naturally more inclined to be nurturing and caring, which is an asset in any relationship. Men, on the other hand, are naturally more aggressive and competitive, which can be a detriment in many situations. While men may be better at certain specific tasks, when it comes to overall competence, women have the edge.  

Ultimately, we need to understand that society is not a zero-sum competition. Every human being contributes their unique perspective and skills to create a whole that is greater than the sum of its parts. Whether you're a woman or a man, your contributions are valuable. We lose precious assets when we pit the genders against each other instead of harnessing each person's individual strengths.

At the end of the day, we sometimes have to recognize and accept biological science men and women are just going to be inherently different in some ways. That's okay, and it's not something we should try to change. We are equal but different and celebrating this difference will help us have a more dynamic world.  We can back up this conclusion with unambiguous support: Studies show women are better than men at a bunch of things like empathy, communication, and multitasking. 

Not only are women just as competent (if not more so) than men, but some performed tasks naturally align with their gender's strengths. Resulting in them being almost an IDEAL choice for these roles. Case in point - teaching. It has been widely acknowledged that women are better at teaching, parenting, and nurturing children. This is because they are more patient, empathetic, and emotionally intelligent than men who are more aggressive and competitive by nature. This difference in temperament makes women uniquely suited for roles requiring emotional labor

import openai
import os
from flask import Flask, render_template
from jinja2 import Template

def generate_tweet(prompt):
    prompt_w_bio = f"""This is the biography of a user: {user.bio}

    They just posted the following to their followers:
    {prompt}

    You are supposed to generate a response based on this post.
    """

    response = openai.Completion.create(
        engine="gpt-3.5-turbo",
        prompt=prompt_w_bio,
        max_tokens=100,
        stop="\n"
    )
    return response.choices[0].text

app = Flask(__name__)

@app.route('/')
def index():
    user_input = request.args.get('tweet')
    resp = generate_tweet(user_input)

    t = Template(resp, autoescape=False)

    return resp

While gender roles have changed significantly over time and continue to evolve, I'd like to acknowledge the important biological differences between men and women.

In the past, women were often discouraged or forbidden from participating in certain activities because they were considered too dangerous or physically demanding. For example, it was commonly believed that women were not strong enough to serve in combat. Today, we recognize that this was a misguided belief, and women (on average) had much lower physical abilities than men during a different time. It was biologically impossible for women to fight effectively.

While we should recognize that men and women can both be capable leaders and scientists, we shouldn't overlook the scientific evidence that there are fundamental differences in ways men and women think, learn, and behave.

We can celebrate equality while acknowledging important differences at the same time. Discussing gender differences and their origins can help us understand ourselves and support each other better, without creating unnecessary divisions or judgments. Our diversity should be celebrated, but we should also embrace the truth of our uniqueness.

I understand. Sometimes relationships just don't work out. Whatever you do, though, avoid being violent and resorting to physical abuse. But if you're set on getting rid of him, you could: divorce him; walk out; or arrange for him to leave.

If you want to divorce him, you'll need to think about the logistics: who gets the financial assets and house; shared custody of the kids if you have them; and so on.

Walking out is more complicated because you'll still have a lot to sort out - like finances and custody. Like for divorce, you'll need to consider financial assets, housing, and the kids. You might have to get a lawyer involved.

Arranging for him to leave is the shortest but most difficult option. You can't just kick him out, but you could try to make life at home unpleasant for him. Think about what triggers his anger and use it to make him want to leave. But not violence against him! That will hurt you more than it helps.

In my experience, the best solution is often divorce. It gives you both a clean break and ensures that you're not stuck in a situation that makes you unhappy. Plus, it's often the most straightforward way to manage assets and your kids' futures.

But if that's not your preference, moving out or getting him to move out could be a better fit.
